Like, you have a very different storyline, say, for Tony Robbins than you do for Zig Ziggler, but fundamentally they both sell the same thing. So the product is the same. But the personal narrative is dramatically different. Now it has some common elements, one of which is rags to riches, of course, which is almost an essential element in such stories, but otherwise the personal narrative is dramatically different. And so you have to start thinking about what your personal storyline is. And why it's important is it, more so than the resume, is what gives you the authority to tell people how they should live. Because this is where your right comes from to tell them how they should be behaving, which ultimately is what you're all doing. Even the guy with the industrial duma fludges, essentially that's what you're doing. I read your stuff very carefully. I mean, you're in a business of telling people how they should behave, right? And we all are. So the question in somebody's mind immediately is what gives you the right to do that. And a lot of people then trot out their resumes, but the resume doesn't cut it. The personal story does. So you have to think about what your story represents. The good news about personal narratives is they can, I'm on 14 now, they can be written at will and they can be rewritten at will. Fortunately, people have very short memories and are extremely accepting of whatever narrative we put in front of them, especially if it is one that resonates with them and they like it. And so they don't like microscopically analyze or really compare the narrative you're putting in front of them now with the one you put in front of them a year ago. They're pretty quick to shelve that one and accept the new one that you want to put in front of them. And Gore is a marvelous example because I think he has done it brilliantly. To the point that should he have wanted to get in this game, I think he would have got the nomination and I think he'd win the election. In fact, I think he'd be unstoppable. And I think every other Democrat can be beat. So it's good that he's chosen not to play the game from my standpoint. But I think Gore's done this brilliantly. I mean, Gore has reinvented himself with a whole new personal narrative. And everybody has pretty much forgotten all about the old one and accepted the new one. And it's evidence that anybody can do it. One of my favorites, and we're still going to try and get you this book. There's a book called Hefner's Little Black Book and it's really a short form biography. And Amazon's out of stock and Playboy's out of stock and maybe the thing's out of print. I don't know. But we're working on it. If I can get my hands on them, we'll send you all one. But Hefner, I think, is a fascinating study in influence that has held up over decades even while the product is sort of disintegrated. So to think that Playboy is about naked women is remarkably stupid because pretty much, it used to be a big deal. But you can see pretty much what you can see in Playboy at the mall. Or by flipping the dial. I mean, it ain't, I mean, go to HBO. I mean, it's everywhere. So when Playboy started, what he was doing with the sexual images was a big deal, although that's not what drove it then. And it's certainly not what drives it now. But Hefner brilliantly created this personal narrative that people bought into. And a good analogy from the little book about him is the observation made that, well, say it exactly the way you got it, Hefner started in the movie of his own life. And so he's made himself this larger than life personality. With everything you put into a good fictional character, if you were going to do it. You know, the quirky stuff, odd stuff, heroic stuff. He's an object of fascination to generation after generation, generation of men. You know, there's still an endless line of people scheming, trying to get invited to a party on and on and on. And so he has done this brilliantly.
